title: Paediatric Procedural Sedation – Weight-Based Dosing
description: A 4-year-old child requires procedural sedation for laceration repair. Practise paediatric weight-based drug dosing, airway management differences between children and adults, and recognition of paediatric respiratory compromise.
learningObjectives: Calculate weight-based drug doses accurately for a paediatric patient
Understand the anatomical differences in paediatric airways
Recognise paediatric laryngospasm and apply early treatment
Select appropriate sedation agents for a brief painful procedure in a child
Apply age-appropriate monitoring parameters for paediatric patients

states.prompt: A 4-year-old boy (weight 16 kg) requires sedation for a forehead laceration repair. You plan to use IV ketamine. The recommended dose for procedural sedation is 1–2 mg/kg IV. What is the CORRECT dose range for this child?

options.label: 16–32 mg IV (1–2 mg/kg × 16 kg)
100 mg IV (adult standard dose)
50 mg IV (universal paediatric dose)

explanations: Correct! Weight-based ketamine dosing: 1–2 mg/kg × 16 kg = 16–32 mg IV. Always use weight-based calculation; never adult flat doses for children.
100 mg is a typical adult dose (70 kg × 1.5 mg/kg). This is 6× too high for a 16 kg child — life-threatening overdose risk.
0.3–0.6 mg/kg is below the recommended procedural sedation dose and may result in inadequate sedation.
There is no universal paediatric dose for ketamine. Always calculate by weight.

checklistItems.description: Correctly calculates ketamine dose (16–32 mg) for 16 kg child

states.prompt: Before administering ketamine, you assess the airway. Which anatomical feature of a 4-year-old's airway is MOST relevant to airway management risk?

options.label: The larynx is more cephalad and anterior; the epiglottis is larger and omega-shaped
Deciduous (baby) teeth are very loose and may fall out
The trachea is the same relative size as an adult
The tongue is proportionally smaller than in adults

explanations: Correct! In children, the larynx is C3–C4 (vs. C4–C5 in adults), more anterior, and the omega-shaped epiglottis is larger relative to the airway. This increases intubation difficulty and laryngospasm risk.
Loose teeth are a practical concern but not the anatomically critical airway risk factor.
The paediatric trachea is proportionally MUCH smaller — a 1 mm reduction in diameter causes 44% reduction in airway cross-sectional area.
The tongue is proportionally LARGER in children, contributing to airway obstruction risk.

checklistItems.description: Identifies cephalad anterior larynx and omega epiglottis as key paediatric airway features

states.prompt: You administer ketamine 20 mg IV (1.25 mg/kg). The child becomes dissociated. The laceration is being repaired. A key safety co-administration with ketamine in paediatric procedural sedation is:

options.label: Midazolam 0.05 mg/kg IV to reduce emergence reactions and secretions
Glycopyrrolate or atropine to reduce ketamine-induced hypersalivation
Propofol 2 mg/kg IV to deepen sedation
No co-administration is needed; ketamine is always used alone

explanations: Correct! Midazolam 0.05–0.1 mg/kg IV reduces paediatric emergence agitation/hallucinations and provides amnesia. Some centres use it routinely with ketamine.
Correct! Ketamine stimulates salivary secretions. Glycopyrrolate 0.01 mg/kg or atropine 0.02 mg/kg helps prevent secretion-related laryngospasm.
Adding propofol risks deep sedation and apnoea in a child. Ketamine does not require propofol supplementation for brief procedures.
Midazolam for emergence and antisialagogues (glycopyrrolate/atropine) are well-evidenced co-administrations with paediatric ketamine sedation.

checklistItems.description: Identifies midazolam and antisialagogue as appropriate ketamine co-administrations
checklistItems.evaluationHint: Patient selected coad_midaz and/or coad_glyco option

states.prompt: Near the end of the procedure, the child develops high-pitched stridor, severe chest retractions, and SpO2 drops rapidly to 82%. No air movement on auscultation. What is occurring and what is the IMMEDIATE response?

options.label: Laryngospasm – apply jaw thrust with 5 cmH2O CPAP (Larson's manoeuvre) and call for help
Bronchospasm – give salbutamol nebuliser
Croup – administer dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV
Foreign body – perform paediatric abdominal thrusts

explanations: Correct! Laryngospasm presents with high-pitched stridor → silence (complete spasm) + chest retractions + hypoxia. First line: jaw thrust with Larson's manoeuvre (pressure at mastoid notch) + gentle CPAP. Call for help immediately.
Bronchospasm causes expiratory wheeze with prolonged exhalation — not complete absence of air entry with stridor.
Croup is viral, not perioperative laryngospasm. Treatment and urgency differ greatly.
No foreign body ingestion is described. This is a post-ketamine procedural complication (laryngospasm).

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies laryngospasm and applies jaw thrust with CPAP

states.prompt: SpO2 dropping to 68% despite CPAP + jaw thrust. Laryngospasm is not breaking. Bag-mask ventilation unable to be delivered. What drug is used to break complete laryngospasm in children?

options.label: Suxamethonium (succinylcholine) 2 mg/kg IV or 4 mg/kg IM
Propofol 1 mg/kg IV to deepen sedation and break spasm
Atropine 0.02 mg/kg IV for vagal-induced laryngospasm
Additional ketamine 1 mg/kg IV

explanations: Correct! Suxamethonium (succinylcholine) is the only drug that reliably breaks complete laryngospasm. Dose: 2 mg/kg IV (preferred) or 4 mg/kg IM if no IV access. This is a life-saving emergency dose.
Propofol may partially relax the larynx but is unreliable for complete laryngospasm and may cause further apnoea.
Atropine treats bradycardia associated with laryngospasm but does NOT break the muscular spasm.
Additional ketamine may worsen secretions and does not reliably break laryngospasm.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies suxamethonium as laryngospasm rescue drug

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• ALWAYS calculate doses by weight in children – never use adult flat doses
• Paediatric airway: cephalad larynx, large epiglottis, small trachea diameter
• Ketamine co-administration: midazolam (emergence) + glycopyrrolate (secretions)
• Laryngospasm: jaw thrust + CPAP → if no break: succinylcholine 2 mg/kg IV or 4 mg/kg IM
